entry.title: From Strategy to Action: Discover and Build Initiatives with AI in SAP Signavio
entry.content:
From Strategy to Action 

AI-Assisted Transformation Advisory: Discover Initiatives in SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager  

Many organizations have a strategy. Fewer organizations successfully execute it. 

The real challenge in transformation is rarely a lack of ideas. It is: 

  • Knowing where to start 
  • Translating strategy into concrete actions 
  • Ensuring initiatives truly support top-down priorities 
  • Acting where it matters most 

With the December 2025 release of SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager, we introduced AI-assisted transformation advisory, initiative builder as part of the standard license. 

This capability helps you move from strategic intent to structured, executable initiatives - faster and with clear alignment to business value. 

What Is Transformation Advisory? 

Transformation Advisory is the guided entry point into your transformation journey inside SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager. 

It helps you: 

  • Bring in a top-down strategic perspective into execution 
  • Understand where your biggest value gaps are 
  • Identify structured improvement opportunities 
  • Create initiatives that are directly linked to strategy 

A key capability within this experience is what we refer to as the Initiative Builder. 

Where to Find This in the Product 

Within SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager, this capability is available as part of Transformation Advisory. 

In the user interface, you will find: 

  • Discover Initiatives - to explore AI-generated initiatives based on your business context  
  • Create Initiative - to manually create and manage initiatives  

In this blog, we refer to this capability as Initiative Builder as well, which is part of the broader Transformation Advisory experience. 

Why This Matters: The Strategy-Execution Gap 

In many organizations, this is the reality: 

  • Strategy is defined at leadership level 
  • Transformation teams run workshops 
  • Initiatives are created bottom-up 
  • Alignment to strategic goals is unclear 

This often leads to: 

  • Scattered or redundant initiatives 
  • Weak prioritization 
  • Effort spent in low-impact areas 
  • Difficulty explaining value realization 

Transformation Advisory addresses this gap by ensuring that every initiative is grounded in strategy and linked to measurable value drivers. 

What You Can Achieve 

With AI-Assisted Initiative Builder, you can: 

1. Bring Strategy into Execution

Translate high-level strategic priorities into concrete transformation initiatives. 

2. Know Where to Start 

Receive structured guidance on the most impactful improvement areas and how to prioritize them. 

3. Act Where It Matters Most 

Focus resources on initiatives that support measurable value drivers. 

4. Strengthen Executive Transparency 

Create traceability from strategy → challenges → initiatives → execution.

5. Reduce Manual Structuring Effort 

Accelerate initiative design without losing governance or control. 

This is not about automating decisions. It is about strengthening clarity and alignment. 

How It Works - A Guided Top-Down Approach 

Step 1 - Provide Strategic Context 

Provide strategic input

You provide business context - for example: 

  • Strategy documents 
  • Financial insights 
  • Operational summaries 
  • Business priorities 

These are simply the input forms for your strategic intent. 

Step 2 - Identify Strategic Challenges 

Select business challenges

The AI analyzes your context and identifies: 

  • Core business challenges 
  • Strategic gaps 
  • Areas of improvement aligned to value drivers 

This ensures your transformation is not disconnected from leadership priorities. 

Step 3 – Discover and Build Initiatives 

Review and select initiatives

Based on the identified challenges, the system helps you discover and build structured initiatives by providing: 

  • Clearly defined initiatives  
  • Detailed descriptions 
  • Alignment to relevant value drivers and End-to-End processes 

In addition, each initiative is enriched with: 

  • Actionable tasks  
  • Relevant assets  
  • Supporting insights 

View generated initiatives

Transformation Leads and Process Owners review, refine, and approve suggested initiatives and recommendations before execution. Human oversight is always maintained. 

Who Is It For? 

Transformation Advisory and Initiative Builder are designed for: 

  • Transformation Leads 
  • Strategy & Value Managers 
  • Process Owners 
  • Program Managers 

It is part of the SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager license. 

How to Access It 

If you are a licensed user of SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager, you can access this capability as follows: 

Prerequisites (one-time setup) 

  • Workspace admins should review and accept SAP’s AI Terms to activate this base AI capability and assign access to relevant users.
  • If you have an SAP Signavio Process Insights license, connect it to your collaboration hub workspace to enable more tailored recommendations.

Steps to get started 

  1. Navigate to the Initiatives page under the Transformation Manager section in the left-side navigation.
  2. Select Discover Initiatives to start a new session.
  3. Provide your strategic input.
  4. Review and generate initiatives and recommendations  

No additional setup is required beyond the initial prerequisites. For a detailed step-by-step guide, refer to this user guide.

Built with Responsible AI Principles 

The capability has been developed in accordance with SAP’s internal AI governance and ethics processes  

Key principles include: 

  • Human-in-the-loop validation
  • No automated execution of decisions 
  • Transparency through AI labeling 

Data Protection and Privacy by Design 

Sensitive information such as names, company names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, financial details, credentials, and URLs is automatically masked before being sent to the Large Language Model (LLM).

This ensures that only relevant business context is processed while protecting sensitive data. As with any AI-supported capability, users should review all AI-generated outputs before use. 

From Transformation as a Project to Transformation as a Capability 

Transformation is no longer a one-time initiative. 

Organizations must continuously: 

  • Reassess strategy 
  • Identify value gaps 
  • Prioritize improvements 
  • Execute with discipline 

AI-Assisted Transformation Advisory enables this shift. 

It helps you move from: 

Scattered initiatives to Structured, strategy-driven execution 

If you are already using SAP Signavio Process Transformation Manager, we encourage you to explore Transformation Advisory and start discovering initiatives that are truly aligned with your business strategy.

entry.title: What does it really take to succeed on SAP Business Network?
entry.content:
Success on SAP Business Network isn’t about simply signing up – it’s about having a solid plan, working well with others, and using the platform’s features and functionalities effectively.

In a new three-part mini video series – Inside SAP Business Network – our team reveals how the platform is evolving, and how suppliers can take advantage of new opportunities, such as AI, for growth, stronger buyer relationships, and increased revenue.

These interviews provide an exclusive look at the people, strategy, and technology behind the world’s largest B2B platforms.

Turn connections into growth with SAP Business Network

At its core, SAP Business Network is about connection and growth. As Mel Sperry, VP of Network Growth, explains, “We are trying to create as many point-to-point or entity-to-entity connections as we can, a vibrant network is one where you have as many possible players.” For suppliers, the strength of the network translates directly into opportunities - more visibility, more leads, and, more chances to win business.

Today, the network is opening up even more opportunities. SAP Business Network now runs on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), meaning its faster, more scalable, and more intelligent. Suppliers benefit from improved uptime and reliability, seamless integration with SAP and third-party systems, and a much more intuitive user experience. With AI-enabled insights and improved reporting, businesses can make smarter decisions while streamlining operations and compliance across global markets.

So, why should you care? Now, you have access to a network, and a team, that doesn’t merely connect businesses but actively helps them grow at a quicker pace.

Watch Inside SAP Business Network with Mel Sperry, VP Network Growth. 

Simplifying complexity through collaboration

Bruna Fernanda Elia-Köenig, Network Strategy Director, highlights how SAP Business Network simplifies complicated processes: “The value… is mostly the collaboration… simplifying their processes and automating repetitive tasks.” By making procurement and supply chain processes easier to manage, SAP Business Network allows both buyers and suppliers to deliver value and create strong relationships with their trading partners.  

Watch Inside SAP Network with Bruna Fernanda Elia-Köenig, Network Strategy Director.

The human side of SAP Business Network

The videos also look at the human side of success. From strategy development to hands-on support, SAP teams are heavily involved in helping customers achieve their goals. As Barbora Kulkoua, Regional Head MEE, states, “Your success is the success of the customer.” That mindset inspires everything from onboarding and enablement to ongoing innovation on the network.

Watch Inside Business Network with Barbora Kulkoua, Regional Head MEE.

Watch the series and start growing

Watch the videos now and discover how to turn connections into growth on SAP Business Network. Get expert advice, and a clearer picture of how SAP is investing in your success. And check out resources in the Community for more ways to get more from your SAP Business Network account.

Thank you for your explanation. We have validated the scenario further and would like to share our observations:

Vendor master (100173) has been maintained with required partner functions: OA → 106582 (default) PI → 106582 (default) Partner schema (Z002) includes VN, OA, and PI as mandatory.

During PO processing: In change mode (ME22N), the partner (PI = 106582) is determined and visible. However, after saving the PO, the partner is not persisted. In display mode (ME23N), the Partners tab appears blank. Table EKPA does not contain any entries for the PO.

Best regards,

Ayaz

entry.title: Re: SBO Mailer Test Connection Failed – Server Unreachabl...
entry.content: Hello, Just an update regarding my case. As suggested, I tested the SMTP connectivity using telnet with the SMTP server and port, and the test failed. After that, I reviewed the firewall configuration and identified that the connection was being blocked. I then added a policy to allow the server to communicate with the SMTP server. Once this was done, SBO Mailer started working normally. Hope this helps others facing the same issue. Thank you.

entry.title: 1H 2026 Product Release - New Applicant workbench OnChange rule
entry.content:
Hello,

 

With the release item RCM-153364:

 

The following enhancements have been made to the redesigned Applicant management tool:

  • OnChange rules support for status field changes: A new status drop-down is added to the Applicant Information portlet, triggering OnChange business rules, alerts, and validations (including mandatory fields and required status transitions) when updated. This ensures all required information is completed before the status change is saved.

The new status field works, and we love it. But unfortunately, the "Move" button is bigger and more straightforward, so I feel that everyone will use the "Move" button instead of changing the Status dropdown field.

 

Unfortunately, the onChange business rule only works if we use the dropdown. But if the recruiters press "Move" button, onChange rules will not be applied.

My question is, can we possibly hide the "Move" button and but still enable the new dropdown field? This way we can ensure that the business rules will work at all times. I did not see any configuration related to the "Move" button. But it would be nice if we can remove this since onChange Business rules does not work on this button

entry.title: Component Availability in Production Lines Fiori App for SAP PEO
entry.content:
[Blog Type: Hands-on Expertise]

 

Summary

This blog post describes an approach of how to implement in SAP S/4HANA a custom Fiori App to check the stock availability of staged components right after the production order release. The purpose is to provide the production supervisors with a detailed view of the availability of the components in the production supply area, before starting the assembly tasks.

The target audience for this blog post is consulting people, project team members, key users, and subject matter experts who are interested in production engineering and operations within SAP S/4HANA.

 

Introduction

The availability check in Shop Floor Control checks whether the components, the capacities or the production resources/tool required for a production order are available. This blog post focuses on the component availability for PEO shop floor orders, but features mentioned are also valid and applicable to production and process orders. From now on, we will use production order as the standard term of reference for wider use.

Depending on the different status of the production order, it is necessary for the system to perform component availability check at different levels: cross-plant, plant, storage location, storage bin. The requirement of the customer was to have three availability stock checking points at different stages of the production preparation process, depending on the order status:

  1. At planned orders level, the availability of secondary requirements is checked for the planned orders.
  2. At production orders level, the availability of component reservations is checked for the created production orders (missing parts evaluation).
  3. At releases orders level, the availability of staged components is checked for the released production orders (component availability in production lines).

This blog post focuses on the new custom Fiori App developed to check the stock availability of staged components after the production order release and before starting the execution of the manufacturing tasks.

Customer used Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) system with embedded deployment option, and the new developed app was valid for both scenarios EWM staging components (storage bin stock) and for non-EWM components (storage location stock). The approach was to use the Production Material Request (PMR) generated during the production order release to manage the information related to the request and the available stock.

 

Availability Check for Components in the Planned Order

For the planned orders, you can check whether the quantity available for components is sufficient to produce the planned order quantity on time. You can carry out the availability check for a single planned order or as a collective availability check for a selection of planned orders. You can carry out the availability check as often as you want and you can choose between two types: availability check according to ATP Logic or availability check against preliminary planning (against planned independent requirements).

The result of the availability check for the components is displayed in the planned order header in the fields “Confirmed quantity” and “Order confirmation date”, which can be reset. You can check the details of each component availability according to the ATP logic on the button “Components ATP” in the header data screen of the planned order. The components checked are categorized into three groups: available components, missing parts and components with faulty check, and each group is displayed in a different color.

Image 1. Availability overview for components of the planned order according to ATP logic.

 

You can switch off the availability check for certain components on the material master data. This is especially useful for materials that you know are always available in stock, such as screws for instance. 

Image 2. Availability check for components on the material master data.

 

Availability Check for Components in the Production Order

To check the availability of the components in a production order, you can either use an overall check (for the entire order) or individual checks (for an individual material component in the order). An overall check determines the availability of all components within an order, and it can be carried out automatically or manually. An individual check determines the availability of a single component, and it can only be triggered manually.

In the mass processing function for production orders, you can check the material availability of several orders at the same time. These collective availability checks always carry out an overall availability check for each of the orders.

The component availability is determined according to the ATP method (available to promise). Using this method, the system checks:

  • Whether the material requirements can be covered on the requirements date.
  • At what point in time can the requirements be covered if full coverage of the requirements is not possible on the date of the requirements.

The availability check can be carried out at plant or at storage location level. This depends on the data maintained in the component and the checking scope. The availability check considers the special stock segment: if, during an availability check, a component requirement exists with reference to a sales order / project, the check is carried out in the individual planning section of the sales order / project.

Image 3. Example of availability scope of check for production order components.

 

On the F2336 “Manage Production Orders” Fiori App you can check if all components required for completing production orders are available or not. You can reset already committed quantities and check the availability again according to your priorities.

Image 4. Availability overview for components of the production order (GUI).

 

Image 5. Availability overview for components of the production order (Fiori).

 

Missing Parts Lists

During an overall availability check of a production order, if the system determines that a component is not available on the required date, the production order is automatically given the status "Material shortage" (MSPT). This status remains active in the order header until a subsequent overall availability check determines that all components have become available according to ATP logic (for example, due to a goods receipt).

If an availability check (ATP logic) determines that a requirement (production order reservation) cannot be totally covered, that is, if the committed quantity is smaller than the required quantity, the material in question is considered a missing part. In the standard SAP S/4HANA system it is possible to display missing parts for a specific order or for several orders using the missing parts information system.

You obtain information on missing parts in the following places in the order:

  • In the missing parts overview, you can check the availability of individual components, edit missing parts data (for example, by changing the requirements quantity or issue storage location) and filter/sort components.
  • In the missing parts list, you can display the missing parts of all orders which have been checked (for collective orders) and also check the availability of individual components.

Image 6. Information about missing parts and availability status on production order.

 

You can use the missing parts information system to display the missing parts list for a selection of components, or for all components. You can also restrict your selection of missing parts according to a specific plant, MRP controller or requirements date, and also with header data of the orders, as the produced material or production supervisor.

Image 7. Missing parts information system for a selection of components.

 

Backorder processing offers the following processing options:

  • You can commit open requirements, for example, if the stock has increased since the last availability check, or a new goods receipt has occurred and so on.
  • Quantities that have already been committed can be redistributed by subtracting an amount already committed to one requirement and assigning it to another more urgent requirement.

 

Standard Fiori Apps for Availability Check

Below you have a list of standard Fiori Apps related to the availability check execution and monitoring processes.

 

MDVP: Collective Availability Check for Planned Orders

You can check whether the components required are available for several planned orders simultaneously. This can be particularly useful if you want to convert several planned orders simultaneously, for example, and you want to check beforehand if and by when the components are available.

COMAC: Collective Availability Check for Production Orders

This is a variant transaction in mass processing (COHV). You can carry out an overall availability check for each of the production orders, that is, you can check the material availability of several orders at the same time. 

CO09: Monitor Product Availability

You can check if all material components, product resources, and tools required for a production order are available on the calculated requirement dates, before you start production.

CO06: Backorder Processing

You can change the product availability confirmation by material, that is, you can commit open requirements or quantities that have already been committed can be redistributed.

CO24: Call Up Missing Part List

The system lists all the production order reservations with missing parts that meet the specified selection criteria.

F2465: Schedule Material Availability Check for Maintenance Orders

You can execute material availability checks for several maintenance orders at runtime or schedule these checks as a recurrent batch job.

F3456: Schedule ATP Run for Production/Planned Orders

 

You can create and schedule background jobs for checking the availability of materials for several production orders or planned orders.

 

Availability Stock Reports for Production Preparation

Customer was a complex assembly discrete industry manufacturer using model-unit parameter effectivity and mainly assembling products with engineering-to-order (ETO) processes. The customer requirement for availability stock verification was to have three availability stock reports at different stages of the production preparation process, depending on the different status of the order:

  1. Availability Stock Report 1 (MRP) with Project Requirements Situation, called DRP Report (Distribution Requirements Planning). At planned orders level, secondary requirements for the checked orders (no components reservation).
  2. Availability Stock Report 2 (ATP) with Shop Floor Order Components Situation, called Missing Parts Evaluation. At production orders level, components reservation for the checked orders.
  3. Availability Stock Report 3 (EWM) with Staged Components Situation, called Component Availability in Production Lines. At releases orders level, checking availability of staged components.

The positioning of each availability stock report and their related process step within the production preparation can be reviewed in the below flow chart.

Image 8. Availability stock reports within the production preparation process.

 

The production preparation process begins once the planned orders have been correctly sequenced according to the requirements of the production lines or positions. This sequence can come directly from the planning carried out by the MRP or from a better adjustment made with PP/DS. To establish the correct sequence in the system, it is necessary to analyze a first availability stock report with the project requirements situation, as main manufacturing scenario was ETO.

Therefore, before the start of the actual production, there was a customer need to have a common availability report which lists all the project’s requirements (also cross plants – cross projects) along with their origin WBS elements and effectivity parameters (model and units) that generated the individual requirements. These requirements were assigned to coverage elements such as stock, planned orders or other planned receipts. A new custom Fiori app was created for having this first availability stock report.

Once the sequence for the planning period is closed, the planned orders for this period are converted into production orders. Once the production orders are created, it is necessary to be able to carry out an availability check of the created orders components stock by plant (own and external warehouses). This availability check should reserve the components for that specific order. This was achieved by using the standard ATP check (missing part concept) for both planned and production orders.

To establish the components’ availability situation for the production orders in the system, it is necessary to analyze a second availability stock report. This was achieved by using the standard missing parts information system report (app CO24).

Image 9. Example of missing parts information system with components availability.

 

Customer wanted to ensure that all the components are located at the corresponding Production Supply Area (PSA) before the release of the production orders. But in SAP S/4HANA, when you release the production order is when the components request to the warehouse is triggered. Therefore, after the production orders are released and become available in production, it is when the material staging happens and components are requested from the warehouse.

Therefore, as customer wanted to ensure all staging components were confirmed before starting the order execution, the following two actions were taken:

  1. Custom checks and validations were included in the production order release process to avoid releasing when missing master data information required for staging.
  2. Custom operation holds were automatically included in production operations when order released, to prevent operators from performing their work after confirmation of the availability of components on the PSA.

Image 10. Example of custom validations on the production order release.

 

To establish the components’ availability situation for the released production orders and at the same time un-holding the corresponding production operations in the system, it is necessary to analyze a third availability stock report.  This was achieved by creating a new custom Fiori App called “Component Availability in Production Lines”, which is purpose of this blog post.

It is worth mentioning that ATP check does not work at PSA level. Therefore, for checking availability of all the staged components, the Production Material Request (PMR) status was checked after releasing the production order. The PMR object is created during WM material staging in SAP ERP and sent to EWM, so staging and consumption for production is performed based on the PMR in EWM. 

Image 11. Example of WM material staging for production order release.

 

Component Staging in Advanced Production Integration with EWM

The material staging on this customer was executed using the Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) transactions and with the so-called Advance Production Integration Interface.

Image 12. Process of supply to production with Advanced Production Integration in EWM

 

Advanced production integration allows to integrate the supply of products into the warehouse in EWM. You can plan the staging of products for production over a period of time, allowing you to use the space in your PSA more flexibly. 

Staging and consumption for production is performed on the basis of a PMR in EWM.  The PMR is created during WM material staging in SAP S/4HANA and sent to EWM. It represents the manufacturing order in EWM. When there is a change to the manufacturing order that is relevant for EWM, SAP S/4HANA sends an update for the PMR.

The PMR is a document containing all the warehouse-relevant data for a manufacturing order. Once the PMRs are created, you can work in EWM independently of SAP S/4HANA.

Image 13. Example of Production Material Request (PMR) in EWM.

 

There are different staging methods available when using the production supply based on the PMR, which are the following:

  • Single-order staging (pick parts): The system creates a warehouse task to move the product to the PSA for each PMR item. Each warehouse task has a reference to a single PMR, so you can see the manufacturing order number on your warehouse task in this case.
  • Cross-order staging (release order parts): The system cumulates products from more than one PMR and creates a warehouse task to move them to the PSA together. The warehouse tasks have no reference to the PMR item.
  • Not relevant for staging (not relevant to WM-PP): You can use this method for products that are already on the PSA or for products that can be consumed from any goods issue bin.
  • Ad-hoc staging (manual staging): You can use the transactions available in EWM to move products which are not planned in a PMR to the PSA.

Image 14. Main staging methods based on the Production Material Request (PMR) in EWM.

 

Production Hold

Customer requirement was to perform the component staging before the order release, and do not release the order until confirmed availability of all staging components. But in SAP S/4HANA, when you release the production order is when the components request to the warehouse is triggered. Consequently, as customer wanted to ensure all staging components were confirmed before starting the order execution, the PEO feature of production holds was used to manage this confirmation process.

Production holds in SAP S/4HANA (also in SAP Digital Manufacturing) are used to temporarily halt production orders (the so-called shop floor orders) due to issues like missing materials, quality defects, or maintenance works. Therefore, you can put an object (production order, operation, operation activity, material, work center, serialized product) on hold if you identify an issue, for example, that is preventing you from performing your work. Once the issue has been solved, you can release the hold.

From among the different PEO Fiori apps, you can set and release holds to the corresponding objects if these actions are available in the corresponding Status and Action Schema (SAS) settings. With the app “Manage Production Holds” (F3101), you have a complete overview of the production holds, including holds that have been released. You see which objects were put on hold, when, and by whom, as well as other useful information such as a reason code or note or even a defect or defect code entered by the user. This gives the users complete transparency over production holds from a single app, including the option to release holds.

Image 15. Example of different holds in “Manage Production Holds” Fiori App.

 

Custom operation holds were automatically set in production operations when production orders were released, to prevent operators from performing their work until confirmation of the availability of components on the PSA. The production supervisors checked the availability of the components in the PSA for each operation of the order and then released the production operation hold to confirm availability and allow operators to start performing work. This task was done on new custom Fiori App called “Component Availability in Production Lines”, which is the purpose of this blog post.

 

Component Availability in Production Lines

The purpose of this custom Fiori App was to display the reservation components and its availability once the production order has been released and the staging has taken place. The idea was to provide the production supervisor with a detailed view of the availability of the components in the PSA before starting the assembly tasks.

The approach was to use the PMR object generated during the production order release to manage the information related to the request and the stock available. Based on the information of the PMR, the new app gathers and shows the information of the stock availability of the requested materials. Some examples of missing parts that can be detected in the production line are the following:

  • Purchased/manufactured materials not on time.
  • Missing parts in one of the components (for instance, a kit staged to the production line with missing parts).
  • Material broken.
  • Defect detected by the operator in the production line.

The new Fiori App was developed based on the guidelines of the List Report Floorplan (Fiori Elements). The different elements of the new app, that is, selection criteria, output list, action buttons, navigation, and others are explained below.

The selection criteria have to do mainly with elements of the production order header, operations and related components. Effectivity parameters were also included to allow filtering by model and unit.

Image 16. Selection criteria on main screen for new component availability app

The output list was structured on two levels:

  • On the first level, all operation activities of the production orders fulfilling the selected criteria were displayed, together with two traffic lights icons to indicate missing components status and on-hold operations status.
  • For each operation activity, the user can select one record and access the list of components assigned to it on a second level, where information about the available stock in the bin / storage location is displayed.
  • On the second level, all components assigned to the corresponding production operation activity were displayed, showing the required quantity against the available stock on the PSA and corresponding staging method.

Image 17. First level of output list (operation activities) for new component availability app

Image 18. Second level of output list (components) for new component availability app

 

The action buttons “Hold” and “Unhold” allowed to release the hold on the order operation activity or set again the operation activity on hold, for one or more rows.

Several navigation options were available to different standard apps, like stock overview, PMR details, or production order details.

 

On the first level of the output list, all operation activities of the production orders fulfilling the selected criteria are displayed, together with below traffic lights info:

  • Traffic light icon “Availability Status”, to indicate missing components status.
  • Traffic light icon “Operation On-Hold”, to indicate on-hold operation activity status.
  • Row color box “Components Existence”, to indicate existence of components assigned to the operation activity.

We included the row color box “Components Existence” to allow operators easily identify at a glance the operation activities (OAs) in each order that do not have associated components. Therefore, green color means that there are components associated with the OA and it makes sense to evaluate their availability, while red color means that there are no components associated with the OA, so “Availability Status” icon will always be green.

Additionally, if the operator tries to view the components associated with one of these red OAs (without associated components), message “No components to show for the operation activity” is displayed.

Image 19. Row color box with existence of components for new component availability app

 

Three colors were possible for the traffic light icon field “Availability Status”, to indicate different status of missing components:

  • Green color: no missing components
  • Yellow color: there are missing components, but under the tolerance.
  • Red color: at least one missing component.

Image 20. Traffic light green in Availability Status icon for new component availability app

Image 21. Traffic light red in Availability Status icon for new component availability app

Image 22. Traffic light yellow in Availability Status icon for new component availability app

 

On the second level of the output list, all components assigned to the corresponding production order operation activity were displayed. The available stock displayed was different based on the issue storage location on the component reservation. For non-EWM components, the storage location stock is displayed.  For EWM staging components, the bin stock is provided. The reference to a sales order / project stock was also considered, that is, the special stock segment.

For the EWM-relevant components, the available stock information to be extracted and shown followed different logic based on the staging methods. The determination of the available stock for the non-PMR relevant items was also different depending on the usage or not of the different Kanban procedures (warehouse kanban, purchase order kanban, and service providers kanban).

Image 23. Example of staging methods for components assigned to different OAs

Image 24. Example of stock available for component with single-order staging method

Image 25. Example of stock available for component with cross-order staging method

 

The app also considered the withdrawal quantity and final issue of the components reservation to calculate the open quantity, just in case running the app for already completed production activities.

Image 26. Example of components reservation with no open quantity

 

As explained before, customer wanted to ensure all staging components were confirmed before starting the order execution, so the PEO feature of production holds was used. When the production orders are released, operation activity holds were automatically included for all the operation activities, to prevent operators from performing their work. This was technically done by using badi WORKORDER_UPDATE and method AT_RELEASE. A custom configuration table was created to enable and disable this action depending on the plant and the production supervisor.

After confirmation of the availability of components on the PSA for the corresponding operation activity, the production supervisor uses the action button “Unhold” to release the hold and allow operators start performing their work.

Image 27. Selection of operation activities to release the hold with action button “Unhold”

Image 28. Release the hold for selected operation activities with action button “Unhold”

 

Two colors were possible for the traffic light icon field “Operation On-Hold”, to indicate the on-hold operation activity status:

  • Red color: operation activity on-hold.
  • Green color: operation activity with hold released.

Action button “Hold” was also available to set again the operation activity on hold, for one or more rows.

Image 29. Display the holds status on Manage Production Operations app

Image 30. Display the holds status for a production order on Manage Production Holds app

Image 31. Display the details of a production hold on Manage Production Holds app

 

Finally, several navigation options were available on the app:

  • Navigation to the warehouse monitor (stock overview)
  • Navigation to the details of the PMR
  • Navigation to the manufacturing order details
  • Navigation to manage production orders and operations apps

Image 32. Navigation options for new component availability app

 

[Disclaimer: Written by Juan Ventura (LinkedIn). This blog post is written by the author based on his personal experience while working on customers with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Opinions and views expressed herein are solely my own. Before implementing any solution, you are advised to always do the analysis and adopt a solution for specific business case only if it suits customer requirements and testing is successful.]

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Integrated Product Development?
entry.content:
What’s New for SAP Integrated Product Development

Welcome to the What’s New! This release of SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD) brings powerful enhancements designed to help you accelerate innovation, improve collaboration, and drive greater efficiency across your product lifecycle. With expanded engineering capabilities and deeper integration across SAP and partner solutions, this update strengthens how teams work together—from concept to manufacturing and beyond.

Collaboration

Email Notifications with AI Summaries

In the My Collaborations app, you now have the option to include an AI-generated summary in your email notifications. This allows you to personalize your updates—choosing either a quick, concise overview or a more detailed notification based on your preference. 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

Collaboration users now receive email notifications for uploaded documents, with an option to view an AI‑generated summary for quicker understanding.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Subscribe to or Unsubscribe from Notifications for a Collaboration. 

Formulation

Add comments when creating a new version of a primary output specification within a recipe

This feature enables users to add comments when creating a new version of a primary output specification within a recipe. The comment is stored with the new version, making it easier for others to understand the purpose of the update.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Creating a Primary Output Specification within a Recipe. 

Qualitative information in the formula contribution view

We’ve enhanced the formula contribution view to display qualitative data (for example, allergen presence or regulatory classifications) right alongside quantitative contribution values

  • You can now add qualitative data columns for composition items—making allergen or labeling-relevant data visible without changing the view.
  • When a calculation provides both quantitative and qualitative results, users can add both in the same contribution view for a more complete evaluation.

Configuration is more flexible: Multiple Quantitative/Qualitative Composition calculations can now be marked as used in the contribution view—as long as they relate to different composition item types.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Configuring Recipe Calculations. 

Product category assignment for recipes

Recipes can now be automatically or manually assigned to a product category, improving structure, consistency, and searchability across formulation workflows.

The Manage Recipes app now includes a Product Category filter, making it easier to find recipes based on business‑relevant categories.

A new Product Categories section has been added in Synchronize Data for Formulation, enabling administrators to upload and maintain the latest category information.

 [Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Assigning a Product Category. 

Formulation API extended to support product category updates

The Formulation APIs on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub have been extended to support reading and updating the product category of a recipe.

View recipe change logs within a recipe

Users can now track recipe changes directly inside the recipe, thanks to the newly added Change Logs tab on the recipe object page.

A dedicated Change Logs tab now appears on every recipe in the Manage Recipes app.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Displaying Recipe Change Logs. 

Automatic workflow triggering based on recipe status changes

Formulation now supports integration with SAP Build Process Automation, making it possible to launch workflows automatically whenever a recipe moves to a specific status.

Authorization

To add and manage workflows in recipe types and to monitor workflows by checking their statuses in the  Monitor Workflows app, administrators must be assigned the ProcessAutomationAdmin role template.

To view tasks in the My Inbox app and execute them (for example, approve or reject workflows created for recipes), business users must be assigned the ProcessAutomationParticipant role template.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Using Workflows for Recipes. 

 Primary output limits in contribution view

Product developers can now instantly see whether their recipe’s contribution values fall within the defined limits of the primary output—right where they work.

This gives users a quick way to check whether contribution values stay within the expected range.

 

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Checking Primary Output Limits. 

Requirements Management and Systems Modeling 

Interactive Creation of Requirement Models with Natural Language Input in Joule

You can now use Joule, our generative AI assistant, to instantly create requirement models by describing what you need in your own words.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 Specification Management

Commenting for specification versioning

Users can now add comments when creating new specification versions. This brings greater transparency, better collaboration, and clear version history across the product lifecycle.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Using Specification Types. 

Synchronizing specification data from SAP IPD to SAP S/4HANA

Specification Management enables seamless collaboration on specification data across SAP S/4HANA and SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD). Using a dedicated Specification Management service within SAP IPD, organizations can synchronize specification attributes, configurations, and data values between systems to support cross‑team collaboration and integrated product development processes.

This hybrid setup enables organizations using Specification Management in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud to seamlessly extend their processes into SAP Integrated Product Development, supporting richer collaboration and cross‑team alignment on specification data.

The following figure shows three key transfer cases that a product developer can use when synchronizing specification data from SAP IPD to SAP S/4HANA:

  • The values from an SAP IPD specification are transferred to SAP S/4HANA, and a new specification is created automatically during the transfer.
  • The values from the SAP IPD specification are transferred to an existing SAP S/4HANA specification, overwriting the current data.
  • The values from the SAP IPD specification are transferred to an existing SAP S/4HANA specification without overwriting the current data.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Hybrid Specific Management. 

 Test Management

Integration of IPD Document Management

IPD Document Management has been integrated with the business objects in IPD Engineering Test Management including Test Cases, Test Plans, Test Execution. With this integration, documents stored in Document Management are now directly linked to their corresponding business objects in the Test Management application, enabling users to leverage the powerful features of the Document Management app.

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

To learn more about the features of SAP Integrated Product Development, visit the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub.

You are invited to join the  SAP PLM Experts Forum  group on LinkedIn.

By the way, do you know how to provide feedback for SAP Integrated Product Development? Watch this video to find out how and send your feedback directly from the application. We always look forward to hearing from you.

entry.title: Re: What’s New in SAP Integrated Product Development?
entry.content: Thank you for this great update 🙂
